authoritative_sources.title: Facilities operations
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public service, support, client-delivery, or customer-workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA is a major public facilities owner and operator. This page is authoritative for facilities-operations governance, tenant satisfaction measures, amenities, and building-service context that shape workplace-service intake and communication.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Workplace-service intake framing and tenant-facing service context

authoritative_sources.title: Manage and buy facilities service contracts
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This GSA facilities-operations guidance is authoritative for national custodial and O&M programs, contractor responsibilities, required reports, quality surveillance references, and the evidence discipline needed before workplace services rely on contractor-backed status.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Service ownership, contractor coordination, and completion evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: National Operations & Maintenance Scope of Work and Technical Specifications
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This public GSA specification is authoritative for work orders, preventive maintenance, equipment inventory, required contractor reports, safety and environmental management, and evidence requirements for facilities operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Work-order evidence review, status confirmation, and completion documentation

authoritative_sources.title: The control of hazardous energy (lockout/tagout)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA is the primary U.S. workplace-safety regulator. The lockout or tagout standard is authoritative for hazardous-energy control during servicing and maintenance and defines when workplace-service communication must defer to controlled maintenance and safety authority.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Safety gating and refusal boundaries for maintenance-related workplace requests

authoritative_sources.title: Obtaining a Permit
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: A city building department is an authoritative local permitting regulator. This guidance is authoritative for permit filing roles, prerequisites, and the line between workplace-service coordination and permit-controlled work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Permit-readiness review and boundary control for facility changes

authoritative_sources.title: Obtain a Certificate of Occupancy
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: A city building department is an authoritative occupancy regulator. This guidance is authoritative for occupancy approval, temporary occupancy, and unresolved-issue handling that materially affect workplace reopening or turnover communication.

authoritative_sources.title: Commissioning Documents: Process, Contents, and Acceptance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: WBDG is a recognized public building guidance platform. This commissioning reference is authoritative for closeout and turnover documentation such as owners project requirements, basis of design, issues logs, systems manuals, training records, and final commissioning reports.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Closeout, turnover, and documentation completeness checks

authoritative_sources.title: What is Facility Management?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized standards bodies, professional frameworks, or public operating references directly relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IFMA is a recognized global professional body for facilities management. Its framework is authoritative for defining facility-management scope, workplace-service terminology, and the people-place-process posture that keeps this lane specific to the built environment.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Terminology normalization and lane-specific scope definition

workflow_stages.name: Service intake and workplace context grounding
workflow_stages.description: Confirm the workplace issue, affected area, service impact, timing, current work-order state, and whether permit, inspection, or safety conditions change the response path.
source_classes: Public service, support, client-delivery, or customer-workflow guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Evidence and systems-of-record review
workflow_stages.description: Validate the controlling work order, service history, room-ready or turnover evidence, contractor or project status, permit or inspection state, and any safety observations before stating service status or next steps.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Supported workplace-service resolution and stakeholder communication
workflow_stages.description: Resolve supported workplace-service requests and prepare stakeholder communication using only documented workflow state, known blockers, and approved terminology.
source_classes: Public service, support, client-delivery, or customer-workflow guidance
Recognized standards bodies, professional frameworks, or public operating references directly relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Exception, repeat-failure, and handoff management
workflow_stages.description: Package unsupported asks, repeat failures, contractor conflicts, supply-impact exceptions, and cross-lane ownership shifts for the correct next owner with evidence and blocker status.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public service, support, client-delivery, or customer-workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Safety, permit, inspection, and closeout escalation
workflow_stages.description: Stop short of workplace readiness or reopening assurances when safety-controlled maintenance, permit status, inspection results, or turnover evidence materially change the answer.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

artifacts: workplace service requests, communication history, and service-category metadata
work orders, room-ready checklists, service-status records, preventive-maintenance tickets, and corrective-maintenance notes
permit applications, permit status, inspection requests, inspection results, and occupancy records
site logs, contractor updates, subcontractor records, schedule changes, change orders, and turnover notes
safety observations, equipment isolation records, incident notes, and operational-impact flags
closeout packages, punch lists, commissioning issues logs, systems manuals, training acknowledgements, and asset or installation evidence
systems_of_record: occupancy, room, or facility inventory repository
document repository for closeout packages, photos, and service evidence

allowed: Classify workplace-service requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Request the minimum tenant facts, authoritative records, and source context needed to continue safely.
Resolve supported workspace, amenity, move-day, room-readiness, or in-location service requests using documented service status and evidence.
Prepare resolution notes, stakeholder communication summaries, facilities or workplace service summaries, and escalation packets without overstating authority.
escalate: Required location, work-order, permit, inspection, safety, or tenant-policy records are missing or materially conflict.
The request seeks a policy exception, after-hours override, financial concession, permit interpretation, code interpretation, or licensed trade judgement.
Repeat failure or cross-functional blocker history shows the issue cannot be safely resolved through another routine service update.
Safety, inspection, closeout, or occupancy conditions materially change whether the space or service can be used.
refuse: Assure users that a space is safe, approved, or ready for use without the required authority and evidence.
Direct technical repair methods, contractor execution, or change-order scope as if the lane owns field work.
Override service restrictions, policy limits, or eligibility constraints just to close the case.

domain_failure_modes: the lane drifts into generic office support language and misses the controlling work order, permit, inspection, or closeout record
users are given misleading room-ready or reopening assurances without permit, inspection, or turnover evidence
repeat-failure history is ignored and the same unsupported workaround is presented as a resolution
service communication is sent before contractor readiness, punch-list closure, training acknowledgement, or closeout evidence is complete
tenant policy and public occupancy guidance conflict and the lane guesses instead of escalating
the lane drifts into licensed trade, code-interpretation, engineering, or exception-approval authority
site safety findings or lockout conditions are flattened into normal service language and the answer becomes unsafe

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Customer or stakeholder requests a policy exception
Service request lacks the detail needed for safe resolution
Communication becomes a handoff to another lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Local occupancy, permit-closeout, and after-hours use rules can vary materially by jurisdiction, building-use type, lease model, and tenant security posture.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The public baseline establishes that permit, inspection, safety, and turnover evidence can gate workplace-service commitments, but the runtime must retrieve tenant policy, access rules, and local jurisdiction context before asserting a project-specific reopening or service-restoration posture.

notes: The research baseline intentionally combines workplace-service guidance with facilities work-order, safety, permit, occupancy, and closeout sources so the lane stays industry-specific.
The lane stops at workplace-service handling and communication. Permit approval, code interpretation, licensed trade signoff, engineering judgement, and exception approval remain outside the lane.
No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for validated use because the remaining variation is explicitly routed to tenant retrieval and human escalation.
